findings.location: §2.6 N4, §3 G7-A test list, §4.1 benten-engine bullet
findings.claim: The thinness test as shipped in the plan is narrower than the one ARCHITECTURE.md (§6 thinness test) and CLAUDE.md validated decision #8 commit to. The plan's `thinness_no_ivm_no_caps_still_works` test and the `EngineBuilder::without_ivm() / without_caps()` surface cover two of the three thinness axes but omit the third — the versioning opt-out. ARCHITECTURE.md §6 test is literally: `NoAuthBackend` + **versioning-disabled** + no IVM subscribers.
findings.evidence: §2.6 N4 lists only `without_ivm()` and `without_caps()`. §3 G7-A's must-pass test is `thinness_no_ivm_no_caps_still_works` (no versioning axis). §4.1 `benten-engine` line says 'thinness test (engine without IVM or caps is still a functional content-addressed graph DB)' — also omits versioning. CLAUDE.md validated decision #8: 'Ephemeral data (presence, cache) does NOT pay versioning cost.' If the engine can't be built *without* the version-chain convention active, ephemeral-data consumers DO pay the cost.
findings.fix: Add a third builder opt-out (`EngineBuilder::without_versioning()` or equivalent, or document that version chains are opt-in-per-Node at call sites and therefore a no-op by default) and extend the thinness test to `thinness_minimal_configuration_still_works` that exercises no-IVM + no-caps + versioning-disabled + (for good measure) diag-off. Also add a test `ephemeral_node_write_does_not_create_version_chain_overhead` that asserts a Node written without opting into the version-chain pattern pays zero CPU/storage for the pattern.

findings.claim: Subgraph CID determinism is asserted ('stores the subgraph content-addressed') but nowhere tested for order-independence. Two developers who compose the same structural subgraph via different DSL builder sequences (e.g., different Node insertion order, different Edge insertion order) must produce byte-identical Subgraph CIDs. Without an explicit test, the encoder can silently become order-sensitive and no CI gate will catch it until a Phase 3 peer sync diverges.
findings.evidence: §2.6 N3 says 'Registration runs invariant checks, stores the subgraph content-addressed.' §4.1 benten-eval does not name a subgraph-CID order-independence test. §4.2 proptest list covers `prop_node_roundtrip_cid_stable` and `prop_edge_roundtrip_cid_stable` but no `prop_subgraph_cid_registration_order_independent`. Invariant 10 (content-addressed per subgraph) is listed as enforced at registration but the plan doesn't specify how the Subgraph CID is computed (sorted Node CIDs + sorted Edge CIDs, Merkle over a canonical topological form, etc.).
findings.fix: Add to §4.1 benten-eval: `subgraph_cid_order_independence` — build the same subgraph twice with Nodes/Edges added in different orders, assert identical CIDs. Add to §4.2: `prop_subgraph_cid_canonical` — random-permute Node/Edge insertion order 10k times and assert CID stability. Specify in §2.5 E1 or a new E-row the canonical serialization rule for Subgraph (recommendation: CID is BLAKE3 over DAG-CBOR of `{sorted Vec<Node CID>, sorted Vec<(source CID, target CID, label, optional properties CID)>, version tag}`). This is the same rigor level Node and Edge already got.

findings.claim: The plan defers Invariant 8 (cumulative iteration budget, multiplicative through ITERATE/CALL) to Phase 2 but does not name this as a compromise with explicit bounding in Phase 1. A Phase 1 handler can legally nest N levels of ITERATE where each level's `maxIterations` passes individual validation (say, 100) but the product (100^N) is effectively unbounded for any N≥4. The bounded-execution claim weakens silently between Phase 1 ship and Phase 2.
findings.evidence: §2.5 E5: 'Invariants 4, 7, 8, 11, 13, 14 are Phase 2' — no stop-gap named. §5 Rank 10 bullet 2: 'Structural invariants 4, 7, 8, 11, 13, 14' listed generically in 'scope boundaries explicitly flagged' but not accompanied by a Phase 1 bounding mechanism. §3 G6-C invariants enforced: 1/2/3/5/6/9/10/12 — missing 8. Invariant 3 (max fan-out) caps horizontal breadth, not multiplicative depth.
findings.fix: Per the philosophy rule 'name the compromise,' add either (a) a Phase 1 stop-gap: a hardcoded `MAX_ITERATE_NEST_DEPTH` (e.g., 3) enforced at registration as a temporary conservative bound that subsumes the multiplicative cap, OR (b) an explicit named compromise: add a `docs/` note and §5 Rank 10 entry clarifying 'Phase 1 does NOT enforce Invariant 8. Nested ITERATE can produce unbounded-in-practice iteration counts; do not deploy Phase 1 engine to untrusted-handler contexts.' Prefer (a). Add test `nested_iterate_depth_bounded` or `registration_rejects_triple_nested_iterate_phase_1`.

findings.claim: The plan leaves unsaid whether TRANSFORM's `expression` is stored on the Node as a source string (parsed at eval time) or as a pre-parsed AST (content-addressed as part of the Node's properties). If source string, that's a minor tension with Claim 1 ('handlers ARE graph structure, not source-code strings'): the string IS content-addressed so it's defensible, but the walked artifact is the AST, not the string, so there's a determinism/claim surface worth pinning.
findings.evidence: §2.5 E4 'Subset of JavaScript... arithmetic, comparison, logical, ternary, property access, array methods (map/filter/reduce/...), object construction, string built-ins, date built-ins, numeric built-ins.' §3 G6-B 'src/expr/parser.rs + src/expr/eval.rs (TRANSFORM expression language)' — parser exists, implying Node stores source. §5 Rank 4 recommends 'hand-roll a Pratt parser with a published BNF.' No statement on whether the Node's `expression` property is the source string, the parsed AST, or both.
findings.fix: Add one sentence to §2.5 E4: 'TRANSFORM Nodes store the expression as source text in a `body` property; the parser is deterministic (BNF published at `docs/TRANSFORM-GRAMMAR.md`) and parses once at registration; the parsed AST is cached alongside the Node but not part of the Node CID input.' Add test `transform_expression_parse_is_deterministic_across_runs` (same source string → same AST bytes). This pins the claim-1 interpretation cleanly without reshaping the plan.

findings.claim: The plan commits Phase 1 to `tokio::sync::broadcast` for the change-notification stream (§5 Rank 2 recommendation) and stipulates it flows through `benten-graph`. This forces a tokio dependency into `benten-graph`, which today has none, and it commits the channel-semantics shape (bounded, lossy-on-overflow) before we know what Phase 3 peer-sync and Phase 2 WASM actually want. Per my dependency-direction mandate: `benten-ivm` should subscribe to `benten-graph`, and `benten-graph` should not know about IVM — but the channel shape implicitly constrains what IVM can be.
findings.evidence: §5 Rank 2 recommendation: 'bounded tokio broadcast... For WASM, a synchronous callback-list wrapper provides the same interface without tokio.' §2.2 G7: 'broadcast channel or equivalent.' The 'or equivalent' is good; the implicit tokio default is the risk. Synchronous callback-list wrapper is an afterthought rather than the default interface.
findings.fix: Invert the default: define the primary interface as a trait `ChangeSubscriber { fn on_change(&self, evt: &ChangeEvent); }` with a synchronous callback list as the default impl in `benten-graph`. A tokio-broadcast adapter lives in `benten-engine` (or a small feature flag in `benten-graph` defaulted off) and registers itself as a `ChangeSubscriber`. This keeps `benten-graph` tokio-free, matches the thin-engine test, and leaves channel-shape decisions for Phase 2/3. Add a note under §5 Rank 2 that this reversal was chosen to protect dependency-direction + thinness.

findings.claim: Pre-R1 triage m8 already fixed the `diag` feature default (default OFF in `benten-eval`) in §2.5 E7/E8. However, §5 Rank 5 still reads 'keep them in `benten-eval` behind a `diag` feature flag (default enabled).' This leaves the plan internally inconsistent on the single decision that protects the thin-engine crate boundary. A Phase 1 implementer reading §5 first could default-on the flag in the wrong Cargo.toml.
findings.evidence: §2.5 E7: 'default OFF in `benten-eval`.' §2.5 E8: 'default off in `benten-eval`, default on via `benten-engine`.' §5 Rank 5: 'keep them in `benten-eval` behind a `diag` feature flag (default enabled).' Contradicts §2.5.
findings.fix: Edit §5 Rank 5 recommendation to: 'keep them in `benten-eval` behind a `diag` feature flag (**default OFF in `benten-eval`; enabled by `benten-engine` default features**). Promote to a crate in Phase 2 only if the `diag` surface grows beyond ~500 LOC.' One-line edit.

findings.claim: C4 (`cid`/`multihash`/`multibase` crate migration) is 'Phase 1 G1 if upstream releases, else defer to Phase 2 and keep our fork-pin.' This is a contingent Phase-1 commitment. If the migration slips, is anything else in Phase 1 blocked? The plan doesn't name the fallback path's impact on T6/T4/T9 determinism CI jobs (they're phrased to survive the migration per m7, which is good) or on the bindings layer's CID serialization. The compromise is partially named; the downstream effects are not.
findings.evidence: §2.1 C4 notes: 'migration is byte-compat, Phase 1 G1 if upstream releases, else defer to Phase 2 and keep our fork-pin.' §5 Rank 9 pre-existing deferrals don't include a C4-slip entry. Appendix A Deliverable-to-Group shows C4 in G1-A '(contingent on upstream).'
findings.fix: Add one sentence to C4: 'If upstream ships late, Phase 1 ships on the fork-pin and every CI job (T4, T5, T6, T9) still passes because assertions are phrased against fixture string, not crate version. No other Phase 1 deliverable depends on the migration.' Add a Rank 9-adjacent bullet listing this as a named named-in-advance scope-slip that does not change Phase 1 exit criterion.
